task: Stripe retries a webhook when our endpoint is slow, and we end up charging the customer twice. How do we make processing safe under those retries?
rationale: Double-charge under provider RETRIES = idempotency/dedup, not the payment API or signature auth.
category: lexical-trap

task: We can't be sure inbound webhooks really come from our provider and aren't forged by someone hitting the public URL.
rationale: Authenticity/forgery = signature verification (mirror of the idempotency task).
category: fine-distinction

task: After we cut ef_search to drop our p99 latency, nearest-neighbor recall tanked. How do we get recall back without blowing latency?
rationale: ef_search + ANN recall/latency = HNSW tuning specifically, not fusion or keyword.
category: specificity-trap

task: We have keyword search today but synonyms and typos miss results. We want to add semantic matching and merge the two rankings.
rationale: MERGE keyword + semantic rankings = hybrid fusion, not bm25 alone.
category: specificity-trap

task: When we update a product price the API immediately returns the new value, but the storefront keeps showing the old price for about 10 minutes.
rationale: API returns FRESH -> DB/replica is fine -> staleness is in the CDN/edge cache.
category: ruling-out-trap

task: Right after a user posts a comment they sometimes don't see their own comment on refresh; it appears a few seconds later.
rationale: Not seeing your OWN just-written data, appears shortly = read-after-write replica lag (mirror of CDN task).
category: ruling-out-trap

task: Two ops people open the same order in the dashboard, both click save, and the second save silently wipes out the first one's changes.
rationale: Silent last-write-wins overwrite = lost update -> optimistic concurrency, not a lock or deadlock.
category: concurrency-trap

task: Under load, two transactions each hold a row the other needs and the database kills one with a 'deadlock detected' error.
rationale: DB 'deadlock detected' = deadlock resolution; 'lock' is a lexical trap toward distributed-lock.
category: lexical-trap

task: Prometheus started OOMing right after we added a per-user_id label to one of our counters.
rationale: Unbounded label -> timeseries explosion = metrics cardinality, not logging/tracing.
category: oblique

task: We need to rotate our JWT signing keys without logging everyone out, and separately stop the login CSRF reports.
rationale: Two distinct intents: key rotation + CSRF; OAuth/rate-limit are lexical neighbors.
category: compound

task: A few abusive clients are hammering our API and starving everyone else; we want to cap each client to a few requests per second.
rationale: Per-client request cap = rate limiting, not a lock.
category: lexical-trap

task: Our GraphQL endpoint fires hundreds of tiny SQL queries to render a single list page.
rationale: Hundreds of tiny per-item queries = N+1; 'SQL/database' is a lexical trap toward pooling.
category: oblique

task: We want to ship a risky change to just 5% of users first and roll it back instantly if error rates spike.
rationale: 5% of users + instant rollback = percentage flag/canary, not an all-or-nothing environment swap.
category: specificity-trap

task: Please plan the seating arrangement and the dinner menu for our company offsite next month.
rationale: No engineering skill fits -> should return no-confident-match.
category: no-match

task: Our model server crashes with CUDA out of memory when traffic spikes.
rationale: CUDA OOM at inference -> gpu-oom; the other two are different OOMs (container, Prometheus).
category: oom-collision

task: Kubernetes keeps restarting our pod with OOMKilled even though the JVM heap looks fine.
rationale: Container OOMKilled -> pod-oomkilled, not GPU or metrics OOM.
category: oom-collision

task: Our Kafka consumers fall behind during peak and the lag keeps growing.
rationale: Kafka consumer lag, not DB replica lag (lexical 'lag' collision).
category: lag-collision

task: Our stream processor double-counts events whenever a worker restarts mid-batch.
rationale: Stream double-count on restart -> exactly-once streaming, not HTTP webhook idempotency.
category: exactly-once-collision

task: We changed our domain's A record an hour ago but some users still hit the old server.
rationale: Stale DNS resolution by TTL -> dns-propagation, not CDN content cache (stale/TTL collision).
category: stale-collision

task: On mobile the page content jumps down right as the banner image finishes loading and people mis-tap.
rationale: Content JUMP on image load -> CLS; lazy-loading is the tempting-but-wrong neighbor.
category: oblique

task: After enabling SSR the console floods with 'text content did not match' and the UI flickers on first load.
rationale: SSR 'did not match' + flicker -> hydration mismatch.
category: oblique

task: Under load our pods peg CPU at 100% and latency spikes; we want more replicas to kick in automatically.
rationale: Auto add replicas on CPU -> HPA, not OOM or PDB.
category: k8s-trap

task: During node upgrades too many replicas get evicted at once and we briefly serve 503s.
rationale: Evictions during drains -> PodDisruptionBudget, not autoscaling or deploy strategy.
category: oblique

task: Adding a new field to our event payload broke downstream consumers still on the old format.
rationale: Schema field break -> schema evolution/registry, not backfill or lag.
category: data-trap

task: We're shipping a new ranking model: we need to shrink it to fit the GPU and serve it to 10% of traffic first.
rationale: Two intents: shrink model (quantization) + 10% rollout (feature-flag); GPU-OOM/batching are neighbors.
category: compound-cross

task: One slow downstream service makes our whole request hang for 30s instead of failing fast; our timeouts don't seem to cascade.
rationale: Hang-not-fail-fast + timeouts not cascading -> deadline propagation, not TCP/HTTP2/rate-limit.
category: deep-oblique

task: After users post product reviews, customers in Asia-Pacific regions see 'No reviews yet' for 3-5 seconds while US users see them instantly. Manual refresh fixes it immediately.
rationale: Task uses 'stale data' language that triggers edge-cache or browser-cache routing, but the ROOT CAUSE is replicas in regional datacenters lagging behind the primary write. The 'eventually consistent' feel is *database replication lag*, not CDN/HTTP layer staleness.
category: lexical collision on 'stale/delay'

task: Two concurrent order-processing workers both update the same inventory row's reserved_qty and available_qty columns within 50ms; both transactions abort with 'deadlock detected' errors.
rationale: Word 'lock' and 'conflict' tempt routing to mutual-exclusion (Redis) or lost-update detection (versioning), but the symptom is explicitly a DATABASE-level circular lock dependency (T1 holds row A, waits for B; T2 holds B, waits for A). Needs transaction retry + lock ordering, not singleton locks or version checks.
category: lexical collision on 'lock/conflict'

task: Requests that traverse a slow geolocation-lookup microservice in the call chain hang for the full 60-second global timeout instead of failing fast in 5 seconds when that service is slow.
rationale: 'Timeout' and 'slow dependency' suggest rate-limiting or connection pooling, but the real issue is DEADLINES ARE NOT PROPAGATED DOWN the gRPC call chain. Each hop re-uses the global timeout instead of deducting elapsed time. Needs cascading deadline semantics, not quota/abuse controls.
category: lexical collision on 'timeout/hang'

task: During traffic spikes, the model-serving container crashes with 'CUDA runtime error: out of memory' when batch inference processes 512 concurrent requests.
rationale: 'OOM' appears in three skills but the CUDA-specific error + inference context points to GPU VRAM exhaustion during batching, NOT Kubernetes pod memory limits (pod-oomkilled) and NOT Prometheus time-series explosion (metrics-cardinality). Needs batch-size reduction or model quantization, not container limits.
category: lexical collision on 'OOM'

task: Kafka consumers processing event-log messages fall 2 hours behind producers at peak traffic; lag continues climbing even though consumer pod CPU/memory stay below 30%.
rationale: 'Lag' appears in both kafka-consumer-lag and read-replica-lag, but the symptom (growing lag despite low resource usage) screams CONSUMER THROUGHPUT < PRODUCER RATE, not database replication. Tempts toward HPA (auto-scaling) but lag growth at low CPU suggests processing logic is the bottleneck, not replica availability.
category: lexical collision on 'lag/behind'

task: Stripe webhook delivery times out mid-request; the system retries 30 seconds later and your endpoint processes the same charge event twice, billing two subscriptions and sending duplicate receipts.
rationale: 'Retry' and 'duplicate' and 'exactly-once' sound like stream-processing concerns, but this is HTTP WEBHOOK DELIVERY under retries. The fix is an idempotency key / dedup table on the event_id, NOT stream checkpoints or payment API logic. Streaming-exactly-once handles Kafka/Flink retries; webhooks need simpler dedup.
category: lexical collision on 'retry/duplicate/exactly-once'

task: A user updates their profile photo and immediately refreshes the page. The page still displays the old photo for 30 seconds. When testing the API directly, it returns fresh data immediately. Hard refresh (Ctrl+Shift+R) doesn't help.
rationale: The ruling-out clue 'API returns fresh data immediately' eliminates read-replica-lag (DB staleness). The clue 'hard refresh doesn't help' eliminates http-client-caching (browser cache). The staleness persists for 30 seconds at the layer between browser and API origin — the CDN edge cache. cdn-cache-invalidation is correct; the edge must be purged or TTL shortened.
category: Cache layer confusion — attributes staleness to browser or DB when it's actually at the edge

task: A cron job must run exactly once across 3 Kubernetes pods. Implemented with a database row lock: when a pod crashes mid-execution, the lock stays held for ~5 minutes before DB connection timeout releases it, blocking all other pods from running the job.
rationale: The ruling-out clue 'lock stays held after pod crash' is the key: database row locks are tied to a connection and orphan on ungraceful shutdown, causing long zombies. A distributed lock (Redis) with an explicit lease/TTL auto-expires. Database deadlocks is conflict detection, not mutual exclusion. Optimistic concurrency is lost-update detection. This requires a durable, cross-process lock: distributed-lock-redis.
category: Persistence & scope — database primitives don't survive pod crashes; distributed locks do

task: A Stripe subscription_renewed webhook increments an internal 'active_subscriptions' counter on each delivery. Due to a 500 error, Stripe retries the webhook 3 times. The Stripe charge succeeds exactly once (correct), but the counter is now +3 instead of +1.
rationale: The ruling-out clue 'charge succeeds once but counter is +3' is critical: Stripe ensures charge idempotency (only one transaction), but the HTTP webhook payload is re-delivered 3 times. The problem is HTTP webhook replay, not the payment API integration or stream processing. webhook-idempotency solves HTTP event deduplication via idempotency keys. payment-gateway-integration addresses charge creation, not webhook re-delivery. streaming-exactly-once is for Kafka, not HTTP webhooks.
category: Event delivery layer — webhook replay deduplication vs. API-level charge semantics

task: A search for 'python' on a full-text (inverted-index) search returns results ranked by term frequency: [python_internal.h, pythonic_utils, python (docs), Python Programming]. Relevance is poor — exact matches should rank higher. No semantic/embedding index exists.
rationale: The ruling-out clue 'full-text / inverted-index' + 'no semantic index' eliminates vector-hnsw-tuning and hybrid-search-fusion (which require embeddings). The problem is ranking relevance within lexical search. BM25 tuning (adjusting k1/b, analyzers, stemming) directly addresses term-frequency bias. fulltext-bm25-tuning is the solution. graphql-n-plus-one is about query multiplicity, not ranking.
category: Search mechanism scope — lexical ranking vs. semantic/embedding vs. data access patterns

task: A GPU inference server batches 4 requests per forward pass, achieving 40 inferences/second throughput. GPU VRAM is 12% utilized. Batch latency is constant at 200ms. Customer needs 300 inferences/second.
rationale: The ruling-out clue 'GPU VRAM is 12% utilized' directly rules out gpu-oom-inference (which requires high memory pressure). Constant latency suggests the bottleneck is throughput, not latency variance. To increase throughput from 40 to 300 inferences/sec with low memory utilization, increase batch size and/or implement dynamic batching to saturate GPU. inference-batching-throughput is correct. model-quantization reduces model size, not throughput when VRAM is underutilized. distributed-tracing is observability, not a performance fix.
category: Performance bottleneck identification — confusing underutilization with memory saturation or observability

task: Rolling out a redesigned payment form to users. Plan: test with 12% of inbound traffic first, measure conversion rate, and toggle back to the old form instantly if conversions drop by >2%. The remaining 88% must see the old form during the test.
rationale: The ruling-out clues '12% of traffic' + 'instant kill-switch via flag' define a canary/percentage rollout pattern. blue-green-deploy is all-or-nothing traffic cutover (not gradual). hpa-autoscaling is about scaling pod replicas based on load, not feature rollout. pod-disruption-budget is about graceful pod termination during maintenance. feature-flag-rollout (canary / gradual rollout with instant rollback) is the only match.
category: Release strategy scope — percentage canary vs. full environment cutover vs. scaling

task: A Stripe webhook arrives with payment_intent.succeeded, you POST charges to your backend, and charge the customer. Stripe retries 5s later with the exact same payload (same event_id). Your logs show the customer charged twice.
rationale: This is classic idempotency: dedup on event_id so retries are no-ops. webhook-signature-verification is about authenticity (HMAC), payment-gateway-integration is about the Stripe API contract itself, and rate-limiting is about abuse. A junior might pick 'signature verification' or 'payment integration' because they see 'Stripe' and 'webhook', but the bug is duplicate processing under retry.
category: surface-vocab — all involve webhooks/payments, but only webhook-idempotency prevents duplicate processing on retry

task: Your search returns 'typo matches' ranked above 'exact keyword matches', even after tuning the embedding model recall. Keyword scores are systematically lower than vector scores in your ranked list.
rationale: The problem is not a single index (vector or keyword) but the MERGE. tuning HNSW won't fix keyword scores, tuning BM25 won't fix vector scores. Only reciprocal-rank-fusion (or custom weighting) re-ranks the combined results. A mid-level engineer might jump to 'tune the vector index' or 'tune BM25' because those are familiar levers, but the real issue is that two retrieval systems need to be married.
category: domain-confusion — all are search improvements, but only hybrid-search-fusion merges two independent ranking signals

task: Two admins load the same user record via a web form. Admin A saves (backend writes version 1→2), then Admin B submits her stale form (still has version 1). Admin A's edits silently vanish.
rationale: Lost-update problem needs compare-and-swap (version column). database-deadlocks is about two *transactions* holding rows the other needs (different symptom). distributed-lock-redis is for mutual exclusion, not silent overwrites. csrf-protection is about request forgery. Only optimistic-concurrency-control solves stale form submissions.
category: domain-confusion — all are writes/data safety, but only optimistic-concurrency detects stale form reads

task: Your API endpoint returns fresh prices (verified in logs), but users via CloudFront see 10-minute-old prices. HTTP headers and database replication lag look fine.
rationale: The API returns fresh data; staleness is in the CDN/edge layer, not the browser or database. http-client-caching would manifest as the *browser* holding old data without refetch. read-replica-lag would show stale data in the logs themselves. cdn-cache-invalidation requires a purge or TTL update on the CDN layer.
category: staleness-root-cause — all cause old data, but only cdn-cache-invalidation applies when ORIGIN is fresh

task: Service A (gRPC) calls Service B (gRPC) which calls Service C (gRPC). When C is slow (500ms), A waits 500ms. When C is down, A hangs for 30s before timing out. You want A to fail fast if B's original deadline is short.
rationale: The issue is timeout cascading: A must inherit B's deadline and pass it to C. tcp-connection-reuse fixes churn/TIME_WAIT, not hanging. http2-head-of-line is about stream multiplexing on one connection. rate-limiting is about quota. Only gRPC deadline propagation solves the 'inherited timeout' problem.
category: network-layer — all affect RPC latency, but only grpc-deadline-propagation cascades timeouts across hops

task: Your ML inference model scores 100ms in a test script, but in production takes 8s with p99 at 45s. Profiling shows CUDA memory fragmentation, occasional GC pauses, GPU at 40% utilization.
rationale: The symptom is memory fragmentation + GC pauses (OOM pressure), not throughput. inference-batching would increase throughput but wouldn't fix the memory fragmentation and GC stops. model-quantization would shrink the model but you're already not OOMing (40% utilization). feature-train-serve-skew is model quality, not latency. Only gpu-oom-inference addresses the memory pressure → GC → latency spike.
category: ml-perf-bottleneck — all improve inference speed, but only gpu-oom-inference addresses memory pressure / GC

task: Stripe resends webhooks when our server times out, but we keep charging customers twice because we process every delivery.
rationale: Webhook idempotency solves replay via idempotency keys + dedup tables. Payment integration is about the API contract, not webhook dedup. Distributed locks handle concurrency but not event replay. Database deadlocks are a different locking issue entirely.
category: All involve duplicate/concurrent processing, but don't address replay safety for retried events.

task: Our OAuth authorization flow works for most users but fails in certain regions—users get stuck at the redirect without receiving a code.
rationale: PKCE prevents authorization-code interception for public clients (SPAs/mobile). JWT rotation manages token lifecycle post-auth. CSRF protects against cross-site reqs. Signature verification is about message authenticity, not the OAuth authorization flow itself.
category: All are authentication/authorization concerns, but only PKCE secures public clients against code interception in proxy-restricted networks.

task: We added user ID to all metric labels for better debugging; the monitoring storage ran out of memory within hours.
rationale: Unbounded labels (user_id) create millions of time series in Prometheus, causing OOM. Tracing and logging have cardinality concerns but separate from metrics. Pod-oomkilled is the symptom, not the root cause.
category: All are observability systems, but only metrics cardinality control prevents explosion from unbounded label values.

task: Every time we upgrade the Kubernetes cluster, too many pods get evicted simultaneously and users see 5xx errors until we scale up again.
rationale: Pod Disruption Budget controls evictions during node drains/upgrades. HPA scales replicas up on load (doesn't prevent evictions). Blue-green swaps entire environments. Feature flags roll out code (not infrastructure).
category: All address availability and deployment, but only PDB limits simultaneous evictions during planned node disruptions.

task: Our ML model achieves 95% accuracy on the validation set but makes poor predictions in production on identical input distributions.
rationale: Train/serve skew happens when features are computed differently at training (batch aggregations) vs. serving (real-time), causing distribution mismatch. Quantization compresses the model size. GPU OOM is resource exhaustion. Batching improves throughput, not accuracy.
category: All affect model deployment performance, but only feature skew causes offline-to-online accuracy degradation.

task: When a downstream microservice degrades, all RPC calls to it hang indefinitely instead of returning an error within the configured timeout.
rationale: Deadline propagation cascades timeouts from caller → callee across all hops. Rate limiting throttles requests (doesn't add timeouts). TCP reuse improves connection efficiency (orthogonal to timeouts). HTTP/2 HOL is about stream multiplexing (not deadline management).
category: All address network/performance concerns, but only deadline propagation ensures cascading timeouts across RPC hops.

task: Stripe webhook for payment_intent.succeeded times out; Stripe retries 30s later. Your system charges the customer twice. Fix needed to prevent this on every future webhook retry.
rationale: webhook-idempotency is the only skill that addresses EVENT REPLAY via idempotency keys + dedup. distributed-lock-redis prevents concurrent execution (different problem), optimistic-concurrency-control prevents lost-updates on record edits (not webhook replay), payment-gateway-integration covers API auth/flow (not webhook handling).
category: domain-vocabulary: traps share 'duplicate prevention' language but miss replay/dedup semantics. distributed-lock-redis tempts with 'mutual exclusion'; optimistic-concurrency-control with 'duplicate overwrite'; payment-gateway-integration with 'payment context'.

task: Vector search recall dropped from 0.92 to 0.58 after switching embedding model from OpenAI (1536-dim) to a cheaper local model (384-dim). Tuning HNSW parameters (ef_search, ef_construction) does not recover recall.

category: surface-vocabulary: traps share 'vector', 'recall', 'search quality' but address different layers. vector-hnsw-tuning tempts with 'recall tuning'; hybrid-search-fusion with 'search blend'; metrics-cardinality with 'high-dimensional'.

task: After migrating from session cookies to OAuth PKCE (Google), some users' login flows hang silently with no JS console errors. CSRF tokens are present and valid. Browser never redirects back from Google.

task: CDN-fronted API: every 1h you purge entire cache (TTL=0). Each purge triggers p99 latency to spike 50ms→5s for 30s, then recovers. Zero error logs; request volumes normal. Logs show no database or connection-pool issues.
